text: The surgeon carefully dissects in the preperitoneal space, closely following the peritoneum to separate it from the myopectineal orifice, ensuring no indirect sac is present.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection of the myopectineal orifice (MPO), clearly exposing the chord structures. The peritoneum is carefully lifted and separated from these structures, with similar dissection extending towards the vas deferens.

The surgeon prepares to place a twelve by sixteen Progrip mesh, ensuring a sufficiently large pocket. The dissection continues as the surgeon sweeps the cord structures aside, revealing the lipoma within the inguinal region.

The surgeon performs hemostasis on attachments to nodal tissue posteriorly, ensuring no further fatty tissue enters the inguinal canal. The surgical field is inspected for nerves, and the pocket is confirmed to be large enough for the mesh to unfold completely without clamshelling or folding.

The surgical flap extends well beyond the area where the mesh is placed, ensuring that the peritoneum cannot slip underneath. The surgeon carefully aligns the flap to avoid folds before proceeding to close it.

The surgeon identifies a small rent in the surgical area and uses a V-lock suture to close it. A 14-gauge angioclath is placed into the flap to evacuate gas, ensuring the mesh remains opposed to the abdominal wall during desufflation.
